skill.name: azure-eventhub-java
skill.description: Build real-time streaming applications with Azure Event Hubs SDK for Java. Use when implementing event streaming, high-throughput data ingestion, or building event-driven architectures.
skill.summary: Build real-time streaming applications with Azure Event Hubs SDK for Java. Use when implementing eve...
skill.author: unknown

security_audit.summary: This skill contains only documentation and educational content. No executable code, network access, or file system operations detected.

content.user_title: Build real-time streaming apps with Azure Event Hubs Java SDK
content.value_statement: This skill enables developers to implement high-throughput, real-time data streaming and event-driven architectures in Java using Azure Event Hubs, providing robust tools for efficient data ingestion and processing.
actual_capabilities: Create synchronous and asynchronous Event Hub producer and consumer clients
Send single events and batches of events efficiently
Configure event partitioning using partition IDs or partition keys
Attach custom properties to event messages
Receive events from specific partitions with checkpointing support
Authenticate using connection strings or Azure Default Credentials
limitations: Requires an active Azure subscription and Event Hubs namespace
Event size and batch limits are enforced by the Azure service
Managing consumer groups and partition ownership requires careful design
Async clients require familiarity with reactive programming patterns

use_cases.title: High-throughput telemetry ingestion
use_cases.description: Ingest large volumes of log data or IoT telemetry from devices into Azure Event Hubs for real-time analytics and downstream processing.

use_cases.title: Decoupling microservices
use_cases.description: Implement an event-driven backbone where microservices publish events without knowing the consumers, enabling loose coupling and scalability.

prompt_templates.title: Create Producer with Connection String
prompt_templates.scenario: Setting up a client to publish events
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate Java code to create an EventHubProducerClient using a connection string for namespace 'my-namespace' and event hub 'orders-hub'.

prompt_templates.title: Send Batch with Partition Key
prompt_templates.scenario: Sending related events to ensure order
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to create an EventDataBatch, add 50 events with a partition key 'customer-123', and send them using the producer client.

output_examples.input: Provide the Maven dependency for the Event Hubs SDK.
Code to send a simple 'Hello World' event.
output: EventData eventData = new EventData("Hello, Event Hubs!");

best_practices: Use EventDataBatch to maximize throughput and minimize network latency
Reuse client instances (Producer/Consumer) instead of creating new ones for every operation
Utilize partition keys to ensure data ordering for specific entities
Implement checkpointing using BlobCheckpointStore for production reliability
anti_patterns: Creating a new EventHubClientBuilder and client for every single message send
Sending events one by one synchronously in a high-frequency loop without batching
Hardcoding connection strings in source code instead of using secure configuration

faq.question: What is the difference between Partition ID and Partition Key?
faq.answer: Partition ID directly targets a specific partition for full control, while Partition Key allows Event Hubs to hash the key and consistently map the event to a partition, ensuring order for that key.

faq.question: How do I handle authentication in a production environment?
faq.answer: Avoid using connection strings in code. Instead, use DefaultAzureCredential or specific credential classes like ClientSecretCredential to authenticate via Azure Active Directory.
